perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to indemnify and save harmless the other party against any and all losses, damages, claims, or expenses incurred or suffered as a result of the other party's breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to indemnify the other party against losses suffered as a direct result of the other party's gross negligence or willful misconduct related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the indemnity clause to only cover gross negligence or willful misconduct significantly narrows the scope of protection, potentially leaving a party uncompensated for breaches caused by ordinary negligence, which is a standard covered under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Brunswick Contract Law, regarding Duty of Care
perturbation.law_explanation: This change limits the scope of indemnification, potentially contradicting common law principles in New Brunswick which hold parties responsible for damages resulting from a breach of their duty of care, even in cases of ordinary negligence. The original clause offers broad protection, while the modification only protects against 'gross negligence or willful misconduct,' a higher standard of fault.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: duty of care | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you duty of care The duty of care is a fiduciary duty requiring directors and officers of a corporation to make decisions that pursue the corporation’s interests with reasonable diligence and prudence. This fiduciary duty is owed by directors and officers to the corporation, not the corporation’s stakeholders or broader society. The American Law Institute’s Principles of Corporate Governance defines the duty of care as the duty by which a corporate director or officer is required to perform their functions in good faith ; in a manner that they reasonably believe to be in the best interests of the corporation; and with the care that an ordinarily prudent person would reasonably be expected to exercise in a like position and under similar circumstances. Courts analyze the duty of care by applying the business judgment rule and examining the processes by which the directors and officers made decisions. Generally, courts do not subject these decisions to judicial review so long as the decision constitutes a valid business judgment. A valid business judgment is one that is made by financially disinterested directors and officers who have become duly informed before exercising judgment, and who exercise judgment in a good-faith effort to advance corporate interests that a reasonably prudent person would make. If the court finds bad faith , gross negligence , or bad processes, the court will subject the directors’ decision to judicial review to analyze whether there was a breach of duty of care. Generally, there is a different standard of negligence for directors and officers for many reasons. Courts want directors and officers to use their expertise to take risks and exercise discretion without the fear of liability, and courts do not want to conduct judicial review of every business decision. Moreover, directors possess greater expertise regarding business decisions than courts. And, it is difficult to objectively review decisions beforehand when the court has already seen its consequences in the present. Limiting exposure to the duty of care: Corporations can limit their exposure to the duty of care through indemnification , directors and officers insurance (D&O insurance), and waivers of liability . Indemnification is explicitly allowed by some corporate statutes . Indemnification authorizes corporations to reimburse any agent , employee, director, or officer for reasonable expenses for losses of any sort arising from any actual or threatened judicial proceeding or investigation so long as the losses result from actions undertaken on behalf of the corporation in good faith and do not arise from a criminal conviction . D&O insurance allows corporations to insure fiduciaries for anything (including crime and bad faith). Generally, however, the insurance market will limit what is actually covered (i.e. criminal activity) and typically only covers losses stemming from good faith decisions. Waivers of liability can limit and/or extend the Board of Directors ’ liability through the corporation’s charter. A corporate charter can extend and/or limit personal liability for director fiduciary duty unless it pertains to a breach of duty of loyalty ; acts or omissions in bad faith; intentional misconduct /violation of law; or any transaction from which a director has received an improper personal benefit. [Last reviewed in January of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex COMMERCE business law corporations wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the scope of indemnification to only cover 'gross negligence or willful misconduct', while the original text covered 'any and all losses, damages, claims, or expenses incurred or suffered as a result of the other party's breach of this Agreement.' The law snippet refers to 'duty of care' and discusses indemnification. It indicates that corporations can limit their exposure to the duty of care through indemnification. However, completely removing indemnification for ordinary negligence, as the changed text does, contradicts the purpose of indemnification, which is to provide protection beyond just gross negligence, potentially undermining the standard of care required.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of New Brunswick.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of wherever ImagicTV deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific jurisdiction ('Province of New Brunswick') and granting ImagicTV unilateral power to choose the governing law creates uncertainty and potentially allows them to select a jurisdiction less favorable to NBTel, contradicting the principle of mutual agreement and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Brunswick Contract Law, regarding Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered text violates the spirit of mutual agreement required in contract law by giving one party (ImagicTV) the sole discretion to choose the governing law. This could be seen as an unfair practice, as contract law generally expects a more equitable agreement on which jurisdiction's laws apply.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows ImagicTV to choose the governing law, removing the certainty of 'Province of New Brunswick' as stated in the original text. This contradicts the principle of mutual agreement required in contract law, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates this change introduces uncertainty and potentially allows ImagicTV to select a jurisdiction less favorable to NBTel. Although the scraped law snippets are not directly relevant, the altered text provides unilateral power to ImagicTV, creating an unfair practice.

perturbation.original_text: Immediately after termination of this Agreement, each party shall return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information and Intellectual Property which was disclosed in, or is then represented in, tangible form.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, each party may, at its discretion, return the other party's Confidential Information and Intellectual Property which was disclosed or represented in tangible form.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall return' to 'may, at its discretion, return' weakens the obligation to return confidential information. This could violate provisions related to trade secret protection, which requires reasonable measures to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Law of Trade Secrets, regarding Reasonable Measures
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered text weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially violating principles of trade secret protection which mandate parties take reasonable steps to safeguard such information. By making the return of confidential information discretionary, the contract fails to ensure consistent and reliable protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall return' to 'may, at its discretion, return' fundamentally weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation highlights a violation of trade secret protection, while the Law Explanation emphasizes that the change fails to ensure consistent and reliable protection. While neither snippet provides explicit law, the overall effect is a substantial weakening of protection. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction, where a contract removes essential information. Therefore the contradiction is significant enough to warrant a 'YES'.
